From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Welsh rugby union club, based in Newport

For the American rugby union team, see [Newport Rugby Football Club (Rhode Island)](/wiki/Newport_Rugby_Football_Club_(Rhode_Island) "Newport Rugby Football Club (Rhode Island)"). For the English rugby union team, see [Newport (Salop) Rugby Union Football Club](/wiki/Newport_(Salop)_Rugby_Union_Football_Club "Newport (Salop) Rugby Union Football Club").

**Newport Rugby Football Club** ([Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language"): ***Clwb Rygbi Casnewydd***) is a [Welsh](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Wales "Rugby union in Wales") [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") club based in the city of [Newport, Wales](/wiki/Newport,_Wales "Newport, Wales"). They presently play in the [Super Rygbi Cymru](/wiki/Super_Rygbi_Cymru "Super Rygbi Cymru"). Until 2021 Newport RFC were based at [Rodney Parade](/wiki/Rodney_Parade "Rodney Parade") situated on the east bank of the [River Usk](/wiki/River_Usk "River Usk").

Every major rugby union touring team to visit Wales has played at Rodney Parade, and all of them were beaten at least once in the twentieth century by a side who, in 1951, played in the match at [Cardiff RFC](/wiki/Cardiff_RFC "Cardiff RFC") that attracted what was, a world-record crowd of 48,500 for a rugby union match between two clubs.[[2]](#cite_note-ParryJones96-2)

In addition to matches against all the major national sides a highlight of the Newport season was the annual match against the [Barbarians](/wiki/Barbarian_F.C. "Barbarian F.C."), ensuring that the Newport fans enjoyed watching world-class players to supplement the Welsh internationals who were a common feature of the 'Black and Ambers'.

Newport supplied over 150 players to the Wales national team and international players to England, Scotland, Ireland, South Africa, Czech Republic, Canada and Fiji as well as over 80 Barbarians.

Following the [regionalisation of Welsh rugby](/wiki/Introduction_of_regional_rugby_union_teams_in_Wales "Introduction of regional rugby union teams in Wales") in 2003, Newport RFC are now a feeder club to the [Dragons](/wiki/Dragons_(rugby_union) "Dragons (rugby union)") regional team.

In March 2017, sale of Rodney Parade to the [Welsh Rugby Union](/wiki/Welsh_Rugby_Union "Welsh Rugby Union") was agreed following a vote of Newport RFC shareholders.[[3]](#cite_note-3) The takeover was completed on 27 June 2017 and work started to install a [hybrid grass](/wiki/Hybrid_grass "Hybrid grass") pitch for the 2017–18 rugby and football season.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

In October 2021 Newport RFC relocated their home matches to [Newport Stadium](/wiki/Newport_Stadium "Newport Stadium") whilst agreeing with the [WRU](/wiki/Welsh_Rugby_Union "Welsh Rugby Union") to play two matches per season at Rodney Parade.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Newport_RFC&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### 1874–1914

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Newport_RFC&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 1874–1914")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Newport_rfc_1904.jpg/250px-Newport_rfc_1904.jpg)](/wiki/File:Newport_rfc_1904.jpg)

The 1904–05 team

Newport RFC were formed in 1874 under the financial backing of the Phillips brewing family, and the playing talents of former pupils of [Monmouth School](/wiki/Monmouth_School "Monmouth School"),[[6]](#cite_note-Smith25-6) a public school which had adopted rugby union in 1873.[[7]](#cite_note-Smith23-7) Newport was originally intended as an [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club, but was unable to find any opponents, but they managed to organise a rugby match against [Cardiff RFC](/wiki/Cardiff_RFC "Cardiff RFC"), and in 1875 played Cardiff in both clubs' first-ever game.[[6]](#cite_note-Smith25-6)

Newport's early success was remarkable, winning every match in their first four seasons between 1875 and 1879.[[8]](#cite_note-InterestingFacts-8) They were also successful in other tournaments winning the first two South Wales Cup competitions.[[9]](#cite_note-Smith34-9) The club's strength was reflected at international level, providing more players to the Welsh national team than any other club in the 19th century, including four captains. In 1881, Newport was one of the 11 clubs present at the forming of the Welsh Rugby Football Union,[[10]](#cite_note-Smith41-10) and provided six players in the first international match played by the [Wales national team](/wiki/Wales_national_rugby_union_team "Wales national rugby union team"), more than any other club.[[11]](#cite_note-Smith40-11)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Rugby_scrum_1904.jpg/250px-Rugby_scrum_1904.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rugby_scrum_1904.jpg)

Scrum between Newport and [London Welsh](/wiki/London_Welsh_RFC "London Welsh RFC"), 31 December 1904

Partially due to their geographical location Newport also played against more established clubs from England and in 1878 a game was arranged against [Manchester Athletic](/w/index.php?title=Manchester_Athletic&action=edit&redlink=1 "Manchester Athletic (page does not exist)") followed by games against Birmingham, Clifton, Gloucester and Swindon. Their dominance led Newport's club secretary, Richard Mulloch to arrange a match against the English domestic champions [Blackheath Rugby Club](/wiki/Blackheath_Rugby_Club "Blackheath Rugby Club"). With a record attendance of 5,000 spectators Newport were out-classed by their opponents, losing four goals and eight tries to nil. However, Newport had played two games in the previous five days and Blackheath brought in outside players to bolster their squad.[[12]](#cite_note-Smith35-12)

In October 1879 Newport played Cardiff RFC in a [floodlit](/wiki/Floodlights_(sport) "Floodlights (sport)") game at Rodney Parade; the first ground to have floodlights installed in Wales.[[12]](#cite_note-Smith35-12)

In 1887, Newport player [Charlie Newman](/wiki/Charlie_Newman "Charlie Newman") was given the captaincy of the Welsh national team, he was the first Newport player to achieve this honour. In 1887, Newport player [Tom Clapp](/wiki/Tom_Clapp "Tom Clapp") was given the Wales captaincy and in 1888, he led the first Welsh side to beat [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland_national_rugby_union_team "Scotland national rugby union team"), a team that included Newport players Powell and Gould. During the 1891–92 season, under captain [Tom Graham](/wiki/Tom_Graham_(rugby_union) "Tom Graham (rugby union)") Newport went unbeaten, winning 29 games and drawing four. Graham brought a new professional attitude to the team, introducing weekly gym training and an avoidance of alcohol.[[13]](#cite_note-Smith71-13)

In 1912, Newport hosted the touring [South Africa national team](/wiki/South_Africa_national_rugby_union_team "South Africa national rugby union team"), and beat them 9–3.[[14]](#cite_note-Smith189-14)

### 1919–1939

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Newport_RFC&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1919–1939")]

* Invincible in 1922–23
* Welsh Club Champions 1920,1923.
* Provided 5 Welsh, 1 English and 1 Irish captains.
* Provided 6 British Lions.
* Golden era of [Wetter](/wiki/Jack_Wetter "Jack Wetter"), [Uzzell](/wiki/Harry_Uzzell "Harry Uzzell"), [Griffiths](/wiki/Vince_Griffiths "Vince Griffiths"), [Morley](/wiki/Jack_Morley "Jack Morley"), [Bunner Travers](/wiki/Bunner_Travers "Bunner Travers") etc.

### 1945–2003

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Newport_RFC&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1945–2003")]

* Golden era of [Jones](/wiki/Ken_Jones_(rugby_union,_born_1921) "Ken Jones (rugby union, born 1921)"), [Burnett](/wiki/Roy_Burnett "Roy Burnett"), [Thomas](/wiki/Malcolm_Thomas_(rugby) "Malcolm Thomas (rugby)"), [Meredith](/wiki/Bryn_Meredith "Bryn Meredith"), [Price](/wiki/Brian_Price_(rugby_union) "Brian Price (rugby union)"), [Watkins](/wiki/David_Watkins_(rugby) "David Watkins (rugby)"), [Jarrett](/wiki/Keith_Jarrett_(rugby) "Keith Jarrett (rugby)") etc.
* Beat Australia 1957, NZ 1963, SA 1969, Tonga 1974.
* Welsh Club Champions 1951, 1956, 1962, 1969.
* Won Welsh Merit Table and Anglo-Welsh Merit Table.
* Provided 7 Welsh, 1 Czech Rep, 2 Canadian, 1 Fijian captains.
* Provided 15 British Lions.
* Introduced 7s to Wales and won Snellings 10 times and R/U 9 times.
* One of pioneers of floodlights in Wales.
* Introduced squad systems / players playing on rota in Wales.
* Won Welsh Cup twice, R/U 3 times.
* Welsh Premier League winners and R/U – twice.

In 1963 Newport, captained by Brian Price, claimed perhaps their greatest victory by defeating [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand_national_rugby_union_team "New Zealand national rugby union team") led by [Wilson Whineray](/wiki/Wilson_Whineray "Wilson Whineray") 3–0. This turned out to be the All Blacks' only defeat on their 1963 tour.[[15]](#cite_note-15) The club was granted [Freedom of the City of Newport](/wiki/Newport,_Wales#Freedom_of_the_City "Newport, Wales") on the 50th anniversary of this victory.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

After rugby turned professional in 1995, Newport initially struggled, being relegated after finishing bottom of the eight-team Welsh Premier Division in 1998, only to be reinstated after Cardiff and Swansea [1998–99 Welsh rugby union rebel season](/w/index.php?title=Temporarily_left_the_league&action=edit&redlink=1 "Temporarily left the league (page does not exist)") over a dispute with the WRU.[[17]](#cite_note-17) After staying up, Newport then moved to full-time professionalism for the 1999–2000 season with the help of benefactor Tony Brown, who financed marquee signings including former South Africa captain [Gary Teichmann](/wiki/Gary_Teichmann "Gary Teichmann"). The first game of the season against Cardiff drew a crowd of almost 7,000, the biggest since the visit of the All Blacks in 1989.[[18]](#cite_note-18) This was accompanied by a rise in season ticket sales from 700 the previous season to 3,300, with a further increase to 4,000 expected the following year.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

In 2003, Newport merged with Ebbw Vale to form the Gwent Dragons side (soon renamed Newport Gwent Dragons) as part of the [Introduction of regional rugby union teams in Wales](/wiki/Introduction_of_regional_rugby_union_teams_in_Wales "Introduction of regional rugby union teams in Wales"). Newport RFC continued to compete as an amateur side, later semi-professional, in the Welsh club league.

### 2003–Present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Newport_RFC&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2003–Present")]

Newport finished the [2021–22 Indigo Group Premiership](/wiki/2021%E2%80%9322_Indigo_Group_Premiership "2021–22 Indigo Group Premiership") in second place, having sat the top of table of most of the season. They did, however, win the Premiership Cup with a 25–21 victory over [Aberavon](/wiki/Aberavon_RFC "Aberavon RFC") at the Millennium Stadium.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

## Club honours

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Newport_RFC&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Club honours")]

* Unofficial Welsh Club Champions: 1895, 1896, 1903, 1912, 1920, 1923, 1951, 1956, 1962, 1969
* South Wales Cup: 1878, 1879, 1882, 1883, 1884, 1890[[21]](#cite_note-Smith485-21)
* [Snelling Sevens](/wiki/Snelling_Sevens "Snelling Sevens"): 1954, 1956, 1957, 1959, 1961, 1962, 1963, 1965, 1967, 1985
* [Welsh Cup](/wiki/WRU_Challenge_Cup "WRU Challenge Cup"): 1977, 2001, 2022
* Welsh Division One Winners: 1990–91[[22]](#cite_note-22)
* [Welsh Premier Division](/wiki/Welsh_Premier_Division "Welsh Premier Division"): 2004
* [Super Rygbi Cymru](/wiki/Super_Rygbi_Cymru "Super Rygbi Cymru"): 2025
* [Super Rygbi Cymru Cup](/w/index.php?title=Super_Rygbi_Cymru_Cup&action=edit&redlink=1 "Super Rygbi Cymru Cup (page does not exist)"): 2026

## British and Irish Lions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Newport_RFC&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: British and Irish Lions")]
